cards.title: Azure Entra ID — The Identity Plane
cards.teaser: Entra ID is not Active Directory in the cloud — it's a completely different identity system built for the internet, and confusing the two will break your architecture.

runs.text: Azure Entra ID (rebranded from Azure Active Directory in 2023) is Microsoft's cloud identity and access management service. Every Azure tenant has exactly one Entra ID directory. Your Azure subscriptions exist inside that tenant, but the directory is its own plane — you can have an Entra ID tenant with no Azure subscriptions, and you can have multiple subscriptions anchored to the same tenant.
What Entra ID is:

items.text: A cloud-native identity provider using OAuth 2.0, OpenID Connect, and SAML. No LDAP, no Kerberos.
The source of truth for users, groups, service principals, and managed identities.
A federation hub — you can sync on-premises AD DS via Entra Connect or federate with external IdPs.

runs.text: What Entra ID is not:

items.text: A drop-in replacement for on-premises AD DS. There is no Group Policy, no OU hierarchy, no LDAP browsing.
Automatically available to all Azure services just because your users are in it. You wire up each integration explicitly.

runs.text: Licensing tiers matter for security features. The free tier supports basic SSO and MFA. P1 (included in Microsoft 365 E3) adds Conditional Access, group-based access, and hybrid join. P2 (Microsoft 365 E5 or standalone) adds Privileged Identity Management (PIM), Identity Protection with risk-based policies, and access reviews. If someone tells you they need Conditional Access, that means P1. If they need PIM, that means P2.

Running workloads that assume AD DS protocols (LDAP, Kerberos, NTLM) requires Azure AD Domain Services — a separate, paid service that provisions a managed AD DS domain. Entra ID alone will not serve LDAP queries.
Entra ID is to on-premises AD DS what a REST API is to a mainframe terminal — same broad job, completely different protocol, and you can't just point existing clients at it without adapter work.

cards.title: Azure RBAC — Roles, Assignments, and Scope
cards.teaser: Azure RBAC controls who can do what to which resource — and getting the scope hierarchy wrong is how teams accidentally grant too much or too little access.

runs.text: Azure Role-Based Access Control (RBAC) has three components: role definitions, role assignments, and scope.
A role definition is a collection of permissions expressed as four lists:

items.text: — management plane operations explicitly excluded from the grant

runs.text: A role assignment binds a security principal (user, group, service principal, or managed identity) to a role definition at a specific scope. The effective permissions are the intersection of what the role allows and what the scope covers.
Scope follows the management hierarchy, and permissions are inherited downward:
Four built-in roles cover most scenarios:

items.text: — full access plus the ability to assign roles to others
— full access to manage resources, cannot assign roles
— can manage role assignments, nothing else

runs.text: Assign at the narrowest scope that works. If a service only needs to read one storage account, give it a Reader assignment on that account — not Contributor at the subscription level.
Custom roles let you define exactly the Actions and DataActions your workload needs when built-ins are too broad or too narrow.
RBAC scope inheritance is like CSS specificity in reverse — rules set at the root cascade down, but you get the most control by being as specific as possible.

cards.title: Azure Managed Identities — Credentials Without Code
cards.teaser: Managed identities let your workloads authenticate to Azure services without storing a secret anywhere, and there's almost no excuse not to use them when running on Azure.

runs.text: Managed identities are service principals whose credentials Azure manages on your behalf. You never see the certificate or client secret — the platform rotates it automatically. Your code requests a token from the Instance Metadata Service (IMDS) endpoint at
http://169.254.169.254/metadata/identity/oauth2/token
, and Azure returns a short-lived bearer token scoped to the target resource.
Two types:
System-assigned

— enabled directly on a resource (VM, App Service, Function App, AKS pod, etc.). It shares the resource's lifecycle: when the resource is deleted, the identity is deleted. You get a 1:1 binding between one resource and one identity.
User-assigned

— created as a standalone Azure resource in a resource group. You assign it to one or more resources. It persists independently of any resource it's attached to, making it useful when you want multiple VMs to share the same identity, or when you need the identity to survive resource recreation (e.g., during blue/green deployments).
The flow:

items.text: Your code calls the IMDS endpoint with the resource URI of the target service (e.g., Key Vault, Storage).
Azure returns a JWT scoped to that resource.
Your code presents the token to the Azure service.
The service validates the token against Entra ID — no passwords, no connection strings, no secrets in env vars.

runs.text: The Azure SDKs support
DefaultAzureCredential
, which automatically discovers managed identity tokens in the right environment, making it trivial to use the same code locally (developer credential) and in production (managed identity).
User-assigned is the right default for production workloads. You can pre-authorize it in Key Vault before the compute resource is created, and it survives VM teardowns.
A managed identity is like a hardware security key that Azure holds for you — it proves who you are without you ever having to know or handle the secret.

cards.title: Privileged Identity Management — Just-in-Time Access
cards.teaser: PIM eliminates standing privileged access — roles are activated on demand, for a limited window, with optional approval, leaving a complete audit trail.

runs.text: Privileged Identity Management (PIM) is an Entra ID P2 feature that changes how privileged roles work. Instead of assigning someone as Owner or Global Administrator permanently, you make them
eligible
for the role. When they need it, they activate it — just in time, for a bounded duration.
The core PIM controls:

items.text: — an eligible user clicks "Activate," picks a duration (up to a configured maximum, typically 1–8 hours), and provides a justification. The role is granted, and when the window closes, it's revoked automatically.
— you can require a designated approver to explicitly grant each activation request. The request expires if not approved within a configurable window.
— PIM can enforce MFA at the moment of activation, even if the user's session already has MFA from sign-in.
— PIM generates periodic reviews asking assigned approvers "does this person still need this role?" Reviewers approve or revoke eligibility. Failure to respond can trigger automatic revocation.
— extends just-in-time activation to group membership, not just Azure RBAC roles, covering scenarios like Microsoft 365 group access.

runs.text: The audit log records who activated which role, when, for how long, with what justification, and who approved it. This is the evidence chain compliance auditors want.
The goal is zero standing privileged access. Nobody is permanently Owner. Activation windows create friction that correlates with legitimate need — and the audit log makes abuse visible.

PIM is like requiring an engineer to check out the production database password from a vault each time they need it, with a two-hour time limit and a log entry — instead of emailing it once and hoping they don't reuse it.

cards.title: Azure Key Vault — Centralized Secret Management
cards.teaser: Key Vault separates your secrets, keys, and certificates from your application code so that rotating a credential doesn't require redeploying anything.

runs.text: Key Vault stores three distinct types of objects, each with different semantics:
Secrets

— arbitrary strings: API keys, connection strings, passwords, SAS tokens. Versioned. Your app references the latest version or a pinned version URI. Rotation creates a new version; the old one stays until purged.
Keys

— cryptographic key material (RSA 2048/3072/4096, EC P-256/P-384/P-521). Used for encrypt, decrypt, wrap, unwrap, sign, and verify operations. The key material never leaves the vault for HSM-backed keys — your application sends data to Key Vault, which performs the operation and returns the result.
Certificates

— X.509 certificates with lifecycle management. Key Vault can auto-renew certificates from DigiCert or Let's Encrypt and notify you on approaching expiry. The private key is stored as a Key Vault Key and is never exported by default.
Access control has two models:

items.text: (legacy) — per-vault, coarse-grained. You grant a principal get/list/set permissions for secrets, keys, and certificates separately.
scoped to individual vaults or even specific secrets.

runs.text: Two resilience features required for regulated environments:

items.text: — deleted objects are retained for 7–90 days in a recoverable state.
— prevents permanent deletion of soft-deleted objects during the retention window, blocking ransomware from destroying your key material.

runs.text: The Premium tier uses FIPS 140-2 Level 3 validated HSMs. For most workloads, Standard is sufficient.
Soft delete is now on by default for new vaults, but purge protection is not. Enable both for production vaults holding encryption keys for regulated data.
Key Vault is a bank vault for credentials — your app doesn't carry its valuables in its wallet, it visits the vault each time it needs them, and the bank keeps the audit log.

cards.title: Microsoft Defender for Cloud — Posture and Workload Protection
cards.teaser: Defender for Cloud gives you a single pane showing how insecure your Azure environment is and, with the paid plans, active threat detection for every major workload type.

runs.text: Defender for Cloud has two layers:
Free layer — Cloud Security Posture Management (CSPM).
Enabled by default for any Azure subscription. It continuously evaluates your resources against security best practices and aggregates findings into a
Secure Score

— a percentage from 0 to 100 representing how well you've followed recommendations. Each recommendation shows the impact on your score if implemented. A new subscription typically starts in the 40–60% range. Getting above 80% requires deliberate effort.
Paid layer — Cloud Workload Protection Plans (CWPP).
Each Defender plan is purchased per resource type and adds real-time threat detection, anomaly alerting, and deep integration with the resource's audit logs:

items.text: — $15/server/month, covers VMs and Arc-connected machines; integrates with Microsoft Defender for Endpoint
— covers AKS, ACR, and Arc-enabled Kubernetes
— detects unusual queries, injection attempts, and database enumeration
— detects exploitation attempts, unusual process spawns, and lateral movement via the web tier
— alerts on unusual access patterns, like a service account suddenly bulk-reading secrets it has never touched

runs.text: Each plan is opt-in per subscription. You can enable free CSPM across all subscriptions for visibility without incurring Defender plan costs until you're ready.

Secure Score is the starting point for every security engagement. Run it first, fix the highest-impact findings (usually MFA gaps and public endpoint exposure), then consider which Defender plans match your threat model.
Defender for Cloud's Secure Score is your credit score for cloud security — a number that tells you how you're doing and exactly which actions will move it.

cards.title: Azure Policy — Enforcing Standards at Scale
cards.teaser: Azure Policy lets you define rules that apply across every subscription in your organization — so guardrails are enforced by the platform, not by hoping engineers remember them.

runs.text: Azure Policy operates on policy definitions, initiative definitions, and assignments.
A
policy definition
specifies a condition and an effect. The condition is a JSON rule that evaluates resource properties. The effect determines what happens when the condition matches:

items.text: — blocks the resource creation or update that violates the rule
— allows the action but marks the resource as non-compliant
— adds required fields to the resource request (e.g., required tags)
— changes properties on create or update (tag normalization)
— deploys a related resource if it doesn't already exist (e.g., deploy a monitoring extension to every new VM)
— marks resources as non-compliant if a related resource is missing

runs.text: Deny
is the strongest control — it blocks bad state from being created.
Audit
is the right starting point when you don't know your blast radius yet.
An
initiative definition

(also called a policy set) groups multiple related policies into a single assignable unit. The built-in Azure Security Benchmark initiative contains over 200 policies and is a reasonable default starting point.
Assignments
bind a definition or initiative to a scope. Effects cascade downward from that scope. You can exclude specific child scopes if needed.
Remediation tasks
handle the pre-existing resources that were already non-compliant when you assigned a policy. For
DeployIfNotExists
and
Modify
policies, you create a remediation task to back-fill compliance on existing resources.

Start with Audit effects across all subscriptions, review the compliance report for a week, and then promote the highest-signal policies to Deny. Jumping straight to Deny on an unfamiliar environment will block legitimate work.
Azure Policy is like a linter that runs against every resource change across your entire organization and fails the build for anyone who violates the rules.

cards.title: Azure Conditional Access — Context-Aware Authentication
cards.teaser: Conditional Access moves authentication beyond username and password by evaluating who you are, where you are, and what you're signing into before deciding whether to grant access.

runs.text: Conditional Access is an Entra ID P1 feature (with risk-based controls requiring P2) that sits between a successful authentication and the application being accessed. It evaluates a set of signals and then either grants, blocks, or grants with conditions.
The signals you can act on:

items.text: — apply different policies to admins vs. regular users
— different requirements for Office 365 vs. your internal app vs. Azure portal
— named IP ranges (your corporate network, a trusted country list) vs. unfamiliar locations
— Intune-compliant device, Hybrid Azure AD Joined, or unknown device
— Entra ID Identity Protection's ML-generated signal: is this sign-in behaving like a compromised credential? (P2)
— is this account flagged as likely compromised based on leaked credential databases? (P2)

runs.text: Grant controls determine what's required to proceed:

items.text: Require an approved client app (e.g., Outlook, not a third-party mail client)

runs.text: Session controls limit what the user can do once granted:

items.text: Sign-in frequency — force re-authentication every N hours regardless of session persistence
Persistent browser session — prevent "stay signed in" on unmanaged devices
Application-enforced restrictions — pass device compliance signals to apps like SharePoint

runs.text: A useful baseline: block all sign-ins from legacy authentication protocols (SMTP AUTH, IMAP, POP3) because they can't handle MFA challenges and are the primary vector for password spray attacks. This one policy blocks a majority of credential-based attacks in most tenants.
Conditional Access is a bouncer with a checklist — just showing a valid ID isn't enough, you also have to be on the guest list, be sober, and not be a known troublemaker.

cards.title: Microsoft Sentinel — Cloud-Native SIEM and SOAR
cards.teaser: Sentinel ingests logs from everything in your environment and turns them into detections, investigations, and automated responses — without you managing the underlying infrastructure.

runs.text: Microsoft Sentinel is a cloud-native Security Information and Event Management (SIEM) and Security Orchestration, Automation, and Response (SOAR) platform built on Azure Monitor / Log Analytics. You don't provision servers or scale clusters — you pay per GB of data ingested.
The four pillars:
Data connectors

pull logs into Sentinel from nearly any source. Microsoft-native connectors (Entra ID sign-in logs, Azure Activity logs, Microsoft 365, Defender products) are free to connect. Non-Microsoft connectors — AWS CloudTrail, Palo Alto firewalls, Okta, third-party SaaS — are available but ingestion of their data is billed at the standard rate.
Analytics rules

define detections. Microsoft provides hundreds of built-in rules tuned to common attack patterns (lateral movement, credential dumping, anomalous sign-in geographies). You write custom rules using KQL (Kusto Query Language) against the raw log data. Rules fire alerts, which become incidents in the incident queue.
Workbooks
are dashboards built on KQL queries — the visualization layer for investigating patterns over time. Microsoft ships pre-built workbooks for each data connector.
Playbooks

are Logic Apps that execute automated responses when an incident fires. Common automations: post to a Teams channel when a high-severity incident opens, isolate a VM when a malware detection fires, block a user account when credential compromise is confirmed.

Cost model: you pay for the volume of data ingested, currently around $2.46/GB (as of 2024). Commitment tiers discount this for high-volume environments. Microsoft 365 Defender data is ingested at no charge for the first 90 days.

KQL fluency is the force multiplier for Sentinel. The difference between a useful detection and a useless one is the precision of the query — investing time in KQL pays off more than any other Sentinel skill.

Sentinel is the security camera system for your entire cloud environment — not just recording footage but running facial recognition on every frame and alerting the guards when it spots a known threat pattern.

cards.title: Azure Security Best Practices — the Short List
cards.teaser: Most Azure breaches are not novel attacks — they exploit the same five gaps over and over, and all five have well-understood fixes you can implement this week.

runs.text: The security posture improvements with the highest return-on-effort, in priority order:
1. Enable MFA and Conditional Access before anything else.

Password spray and phishing are the dominant initial access vectors. MFA blocks the vast majority of them. Start with a Conditional Access policy requiring MFA for all users accessing the Azure portal and Microsoft 365. If you have P1, add a named location exclusion for your corporate network. This takes two hours and eliminates the largest attack surface.
2. Apply least privilege everywhere.
Never give a workload subscription Owner. Create a custom role or use the narrowest built-in role that gets the job done. If a Function App reads from one storage container, give it
Storage Blob Data Reader
on that container — not Contributor on the subscription. Audit your role assignments quarterly.
3. Rotate secrets via Key Vault versioning, not by redeploying.

If your app has connection strings in environment variables, you rotate them by redeploying. If your app reads from Key Vault at runtime, you rotate by creating a new secret version and restarting the app (or using Key Vault references, which pick up the new version automatically). No emergency deploys at 2 AM.
4. Use Managed Identities for workload authentication.
If your code has a client secret in a config file or env var, replace it with a managed identity and
DefaultAzureCredential
. There is no legitimate reason for a compute resource running on Azure to use a static credential to reach another Azure service.
5. Monitor your Defender for Cloud Secure Score.

It surfaces your specific misconfigurations. A score below 70% in a production subscription means there are known, fixable gaps. Treat findings above "high" severity the same way you treat high-severity bugs.
Run
az security secure-score-controls list --subscription <id>
to pull your current Secure Score and top recommendations in one command — useful for a quick audit before a security review.

Securing an Azure environment is like locking your house: most break-ins use the unlocked front door, not a sophisticated bypass — so the first step is just making sure the door is locked, not installing a vault.
